items.feature: real grill and skewer proof visibility
items.definition: Measures whether the storyboard puts the real grill, hero skewers, fresh handoff, and actual table use early instead of relying on decor, neon, or generic food glamour.
high_score_signals: real grill or skewer handoff appears in the first 3 shots
the viewer can connect grill, product, and table context quickly
the store feels like a real operating venue instead of a visual mood board
low_score_signals: opening is mostly ambience and environment
hero food looks like stock or static plate footage
the audience cannot tell whether the product is real and on sale
items.score_rule: The earlier and denser the real grill proof appears, the higher the score.

items.definition: Measures whether the cover direction matches the true reason to visit the barbecue shop, such as hero skewers, charcoal heat, seafood freshness, night traffic, social tables, or visible value.
high_score_signals: title and image focus on one concrete promise
the audience can tell what barbecue choice problem the video solves
cover and opening shot reinforce the same message
low_score_signals: cover is atmospheric but vague
title says skewers while the image shows only environment
the promise is generic nightlife instead of a store-specific reason
items.score_rule: The more clearly the cover answers why the viewer should come here, the higher the score.

items.definition: Measures whether the plan shows real heat signals such as charcoal glow, turning, seasoning, smoke, grill sound, and fresh-off-grill motion.
high_score_signals: continuous process from grill control to table handoff
heat footage matches the product later shown at the table
the plan does not rely on fake fire or post-produced smoke
low_score_signals: static beauty shots stand in for live grilling
fire or smoke feels synthetic or decorative
the grill stage and the served product do not match
items.score_rule: The more viewers can feel that the skewer just left the grill, the higher the score.

items.definition: Measures whether freshness or made-to-order claims are supported by a readable chain from item prep or display through grill stage to handoff and bite.
high_score_signals: at least three linked stages are visible
the same hero item remains identifiable across stages
the chain feels verifiable instead of patched together
low_score_signals: prep footage never reaches the grill or the table
multiple unrelated items are edited into one fake chain
freshness claims exist only in text or narration
items.score_rule: The more complete and verifiable the item chain is, the higher the score.

items.definition: Measures whether the plan uses real night-service signals such as storefront movement, occupied seating, refills, add-on orders, drinks, and leftover skewers to prove business state.
high_score_signals: real night-service occupancy and service rhythm are visible
the storyboard clearly identifies the service window
business proof ties back to the real storefront and table layout
low_score_signals: empty tables are edited to fake density
business claims are mostly voice-over
customer shots and store space do not connect
items.score_rule: The clearer and more verifiable the night-service state is, the higher the score.

items.definition: Measures whether the storyboard fits real constraints such as service timing, grill access, outdoor seating permission, customer visibility rules, smoke, weather, team size, and shoot duration.
high_score_signals: critical shots match realistic operating windows
permissions and safety boundaries are surfaced early
the team can execute the plan without obviously disrupting service
low_score_signals: the plan assumes unrestricted fire, faces, and peak-hour access
too many locations or service beats are packed into a short shoot
camera movement and business flow conflict directly
items.score_rule: The more directly the plan can be executed during real service, the higher the score.

items.definition: Measures whether the plan names the exact hero skewers, tables, service beats, grill access, seating zones, and permissions required to deliver the storyboard.
high_score_signals: hero shots map to specific items and locations
pickup needs are concrete rather than vague
missing assets have explicit replacement routes
low_score_signals: dependencies are described only as generic store footage
the plan assumes any item can be recreated on demand
face, fire, weather, or outdoor constraints are omitted
items.score_rule: The more specific and execution-ready the dependencies are, the higher the score.

items.feature: honest downgrade when proof is missing
items.definition: Measures whether the storyboard honestly shifts to a smaller but true route when the store lacks crowd proof, grill access, seafood prep proof, or social-table footage.
high_score_signals: missing proof layers are clearly named
the execution score drops when proof drops
the downgraded route still feels like barbecue rather than generic food content
low_score_signals: high confidence remains despite missing proof
external diners or external grill footage fill the gap
the fallback still pretends to be a hot-traffic exploration video
items.score_rule: The more honestly the plan protects deliverability under weaker assets, the higher the score.
